task: R4-B — Federal employment/labor baseline (DOL FLSA, OSHA, EEOC hiring)
disclaimer: Operational employment/labor compliance research and cited public-records compilation — not legal advice. Confirm wage-hour, OSHA, EEOC, and state labor obligations with licensed counsel and the relevant agency before relying on any fact for operations or filings.
policy: Public .gov / eCFR sources only; HTTP 403 = park; gaps explicit.

cited_facts.text: The Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA) establishes federal minimum wage, overtime pay, recordkeeping, and youth-employment standards for covered employees in the private and public sectors.

citation.source: DOL WHD — Fair Labor Standards Act overview
citation.agency: U.S. Department of Labor — Wage and Hour Division

cited_facts.text: Covered nonexempt employees must receive overtime pay at not less than one and one-half times the regular rate of pay for hours worked over 40 in a workweek unless an exemption applies.
Executive, administrative, and professional exemptions (and certain computer/outside-sales employees) require meeting salary and duties tests codified at 29 CFR Part 541.

citation.source: eCFR — 29 CFR Part 541 (Defining and Delimiting Exemptions)

cited_facts.text: Employers must keep payroll records, hours worked each day, and total overtime earnings for nonexempt workers; records must be preserved as required by 29 CFR Part 516.

citation.source: eCFR — 29 CFR Part 516 (Records to be Kept by Employers)

cited_facts.text: Child-labor provisions restrict hours and occupations for workers under 18; hazardous occupation orders apply to employment in construction, manufacturing, and other listed sectors.
The Occupational Safety and Health Act requires employers to furnish employment free from recognized hazards likely to cause death or serious physical harm (General Duty Clause, Section 5(a)(1)).

citation.source: OSHA — Occupational Safety and Health Act of 1970

cited_facts.text: OSHA standards in 29 CFR Part 1910 govern general industry hazards (machine guarding, hazard communication, walking-working surfaces, personal protective equipment).
Construction employers must comply with 29 CFR Part 1926 (fall protection, scaffolding, excavation, steel erection) in addition to the General Duty Clause.
Employers covered by the OSH Act must display the OSHA Job Safety and Health poster (or state-plan equivalent) informing workers of rights and how to report hazards.
Employers must report work-related fatalities within 8 hours and in-patient hospitalizations, amputations, or eye losses within 24 hours to OSHA.

citation.source: OSHA — Reporting fatalities and severe injuries

cited_facts.text: Title VII of the Civil Rights Act prohibits employment discrimination based on race, color, religion, sex (including pregnancy), or national origin in hiring, firing, promotion, and compensation.

citation.source: EEOC — Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964

cited_facts.text: The Americans with Disabilities Act (Title I) prohibits covered employers from discriminating against qualified individuals with disabilities in job application procedures, hiring, and other terms of employment.

citation.source: EEOC — Americans with Disabilities Act of 1990

cited_facts.text: The Age Discrimination in Employment Act protects applicants and employees 40 and older from discrimination in hiring, discharge, compensation, and terms of employment.

citation.source: EEOC — Age Discrimination in Employment Act of 1967

cited_facts.text: Covered employers must post notices describing federal laws prohibiting job discrimination (EEOC 'Know Your Rights' poster / 'EEO Is The Law').
Unlawful pre-employment inquiries include those that disproportionately screen out protected groups; criminal history and credit checks may require individualized assessment under EEOC guidance.

citation.source: EEOC — Employment tests and selection procedures
